summary: Stancil argues that conferences rarely deliver real learning or authentic networking, often devolving into performative talks and repetitive social rituals. Still, he says they have a hidden value: they let you see many plausible versions of your future career all at once, helping you choose intentionally among finite paths.
money_quotes: The presenters aren’t there to teach, but to be recognized and celebrated; the audience isn’t there to become enlightened, but to feel enlightened.
Conferences aren’t just our professional connections and friends, all in one place; they’re also our parallel lives, all at one time.
That’s what conferences are for: For showing us how wide-ranging a profession can be, while reminding us it is not infinite.
If necessity is the mother of invention, boundaries are the mother of intention.
So as you leave, open the plane’s window. Look back; pay attention. Who are you becoming? Who do you want to become?
key_insight: Conferences are less about learning or networking than about revealing the limited set of career trajectories you’re likely to follow, so you can choose one deliberately.
